summary: claw-subagent-service installs a persistent C2 backdoor with continuous workspace exfiltration and auto-updating foothold
details: On install, this package establishes a publisher-controlled remote-administration channel on the installer's host. On Windows, scripts/post-install.js silently registers a SYSTEM-level Windows service (node-windows, auto-start, crash-restart) running service/daemon.js with no interactive consent when npm install is run from an administrator shell. The daemon connects to RongCloud IM using a session token fetched from the publisher's server (worker.js: hardcoded serverUrl https://newsradar.dreamdt.cn/im and RongCloud appKey bmdehs6pbyyks) and listens for messages from the publisher's IM account 'guardserver' (rongyun-message-sender.js: this.serverImId = 'guardserver'). The message handler (rongyun-message-handler.js lines 71-95) dispatches remote COMMAND (start/stop/restart local services), CHAT_MESSAGE (forward arbitrary content to a local AI), DEVICE_CONTROL (disable / enable / uninstall-self / reinstall), and CREATE/DELETE_OPENCODE_SESSION operations — i.e., remote command execution under the installer's identity (SYSTEM on Windows). Concurrently, heartbeat-dashboard.js runs setInterval every 30 seconds to collect and POST workspace telemetry to 'guardserver': OpenClaw session transcripts and conversation context from ~/.openclaw/agents/<agent>/sessions/*.jsonl, per-prompt usage events (token counts, costs, model/provider/timestamps), project and task lists, OS platform/arch/CPU/memory/uptime, and MAC address. updater.js polls the npm registry every 6 hours and runs `npm install -g claw-subagent-service@<latest>` then restarts the worker, so the publisher can push arbitrary new code to every running install within 6 hours with no user prompt and no version pin. The combination — silent SYSTEM-service persistence, hardcoded publisher C2, structured remote-command dispatch including self-uninstall, continuous prompt/cost/project exfiltration, and an auto-rotating code base — is an unambiguous remote-administration backdoor regardless of the publisher's stated intent.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
